findings.description: fetch_events runs an unbounded query with no LIMIT/pagination and materializes the full result via .fetchall() into a list; an arbitrarily wide date range (months/years) loads the entire events-with-users join into memory, and render_activity_report then builds the whole HTML string on top of it, doubling the memory hit for large ranges.

render_activity_report builds the rows string via repeated 'rows += f"..."' in a loop (textbook quadratic string concatenation; CPython's in-place += optimization is not guaranteed) instead of appending to a list and using ''.join() or a generator, so HTML assembly cost grows super-linearly with event count.

Every method filters events on created_at (>=, <) and joins on events.user_id; if those columns lack indexes (schema is out of scope and therefore unverifiable) each call is a full table scan plus a scan-based join, degrading linearly with table size — likely the dominant cost at scale, but confidence is capped because no schema/index definition is in scope.

render_activity_report holds the entire row list and the fully-rendered HTML page in memory before returning, with no streaming path to save_report; for large reports a generator-based fetch + chunked write would bound peak memory instead of buffering the whole document.

_connect opens a brand-new sqlite3 connection on every public-method call with no pooling/reuse; for a web service serving concurrent report requests this adds per-call connection setup, though impact is modest given SQLite connections are cheap and SQLite serializes writes anyway.

event_count issues a dedicated COUNT(*) query while render_activity_report already derives the same total via len(events); a caller needing both the rendered report and the count for one date range pays for two scans of the same range (cross-entry-point redundancy, not a single-call defect).
